papers.title: Jet interactions with a giant molecular cloud in the Galactic centre and ejection of hypervelocity stars
papers.author_names: Joseph Silk Vincenzo Antonuccio-Delogu Yohan Dubois Volker Gaibler Marcel R. Haas Sadegh Khochfar Martin Krause

abstract.text: The hypervelocity OB stars in the Milky Way Galaxy were ejected from the central regions some 10-100 million years ago. We argue that these stars, {as well as many more abundant bound OB stars in the innermost few parsecs,} were generated by the interactions of an AGN jet from the central black hole with a dense molecular cloud. Considerations of the associated energy and momentum injection have broader implications for the possible origin of the Fermi bubbles and for the enrichment of the intergalactic medium.

brief.tldr: [research paper] The paper proposes that hypervelocity OB stars in the Milky Way were ejected not by binary interactions or black hole kicks, but by interactions between past active galactic nucleus (AGN) jets and dense molecular clouds in the Galactic center. These jet-cloud collisions transfer momentum to gas clumps, triggering star formation and, in rare cases, ejecting massive stars at hypervelocity. The model links this mechanism to the observed Fermi bubbles and explains the energetics of star ejection through momentum conservation, with simulations suggesting that 10% or more of jet energy can accelerate gas to escape velocity over tens of millions of years, while accounting for cloud self-gravity and star formation efficiency in dense environments.

source_fields.title: Jet interactions with a giant molecular cloud in the Galactic centre and ejection of hypervelocity stars

source_fields.sections_text: The model: The paper proposes a model in which AGN-driven jet interactions with a dense molecular cloud at the Galactic Center trigger hypervelocity star ejection via momentum transfer from a jet-driven blast wave, with the core idea that the expanding bow shock enhances momentum flux sufficiently to eject a small fraction of OB stars at high velocities, consistent with observed ejection efficiencies and momentum budgets. Introduction: This paper proposes a novel mechanism in which past recurrent AGN activity in the Milky Way's center, via jet-induced gas outflows and star formation, could have ejected hypervelocity stars by accelerating stellar remnants through energetic feedback, addressing the long-standing problem of explaining the high velocities and recent origin of these stars beyond binary ejection or black hole scattering. Cosmological implications: Jet-induced ejection of massive stars from galactic nuclei enriches the intergalactic medium with α-enhanced, low-metallicity material, with implications for chemical evolution, hypervelocity star formation, and large-scale cosmic structures. Star formation in the Galactic centre: Star formation in the Galactic center is driven by a combination of jet-induced gas compression, a top-heavy initial mass function in young clusters, and the presence of a massive circumnuclear molecular gas disk, leading to the formation of young, massive clusters like the Arches and the production of hypervelocity stars through jet-driven feedback.

title: Jet interactions with a giant molecular cloud in the Galactic centre and ejection of hypervelocity stars

catalog_assessment.evidence: Purely theoretical paper proposing jet-cloud interaction mechanism for hypervelocity star formation. No observational data or object-level catalog presented.

papers.title: Catch me if you can: is there a runaway-mass black hole in the Orion Nebula Cluster?
papers.author_names: Ladislav Subr Pavel Kroupa Holger Baumgardt

abstract.text: We investigate the dynamical evolution of the Orion Nebula Cluster (ONC) by means of direct N-body integrations. A large fraction of residual gas was probably expelled when the ONC formed, so we assume that the ONC was much more compact when it formed compared to its current size, in agreement with the embedded cluster radius-mass relation from Marks & Kroupa (2012). Hence, we assume that few-body relaxation played an important role during the initial phase of evolution of the ONC. In particular, three body interactions among OB stars likely led to their ejection from the cluster and, at the same time, to the formation of a massive object via runaway physical stellar collisions. The resulting depletion of the high mass end of the stellar mass function in the cluster is one of the important points where our models fit the observational data. We speculate that the runaway-mass star may have collapsed directly into a massive black hole (Mbh > 100Msun). Such a dark object could explain the large velocity dispersion of the four Trapezium stars observed in the ONC core. We further show that the putative massive black hole is likely to be a member of a binary system with appr. 70 per cent probability. In such a case, it could be detected either due to short periods of enhanced accretion of stellar winds from the secondary star during pericentre passages, or through a measurement of the motion of the secondary whose velocity would exceed 10 km/s along the whole orbit.

source_fields.sections_text: 2. Model: The paper proposes a numerical N-body simulation approach using modified NBODY6 to model the gravitational evolution of stellar clusters, incorporating gas dynamics with radiation pressure or instantaneous expulsion and adopting a Kroupa initial mass function with finite stellar radii and simplified mass binning to track cluster evolution over 2.5 Myr. 3. Discussion: The experiment finds a clear $N_{\\mathrm{OB}}$–$M_{\\bullet}$ anticorrelation in the final states of the models, with a mean runaway-mass black hole mass of approximately 140–170 $M_{\\odot}$ and OB star counts ranging from 20 to 35, consistent with observed properties of the Orion Nebula Cluster. 4. Conclusions: The dynamical evolution of the ONC, including frequent massive star interactions and potential formation of a $\\gtrsim 100M_{\\odot}$ black hole remnant, supports a compact, relaxation-dominated history and offers insights into massive star evolution and cluster dynamics. 1. Introduction: This paper presents a novel dynamical model showing how initially compact star clusters evolve into the observed state of the Orion Nebula Cluster through violent interactions, explaining the supervirial Trapezium and the deficit of OB stars via early dynamical ejections and stellar disruptions. Appendix A Theoretical predictions: This section presents a theoretical framework for estimating the rate of high-velocity ejections and collisions of massive stars in star clusters via three-body scattering, based on gravitational focusing and binary interaction dynamics.

catalog_assessment.evidence: Dynamical simulation study of Orion Nebula Cluster evolution using N-body integrations. Paper speculates about possible black hole but presents no observational stellar data or catalog.
